metrics.narrative: Rompetrol articulated the operational pain driving this evaluation but has not yet quantified the full financial impact in a board-ready business case. On the 27 Oct 2025 intro call, Meiramgul Kusherbayeva (Manager of Procurement) described chaotic intake wasting around 40% of time on non-value tasks, fragmented data silos across procurement/sourcing/invoicing creating multiple failure points, and high manual effort with frustrated users. Caroline quantified tail-spend inefficiency generically as '40% of time on non-value tasks' (intro call, 27 Oct 2025). The 24 Nov 2025 demo noted 'heavy tail-spend sourcing of low-value, high-volume items consuming significant manual effort' and 'limited visibility and control over spend across 20+ affiliated companies' (demo notes, 24 Nov 2025). On 27 Mar 2026, Asmita presented a conservative ROI model projecting €6M savings in year one, cumulative ~€30M by year three, and best-case improvement rates of 2.5-3.5% across indirect categories (ROI call, 27 Mar 2026). Meiramgul confirmed they will 'monitor KPIs and report to management quarterly or monthly' and select 'scalable KPIs for our industry' (27 Mar 2026). However, as of the 28 May 2026 call, Meiramgul stated the business case and RFI form are still being finalized internally ('I will send edify form in 2-3 weeks', 28 May 2026) and the senior management has not yet approved the investment. The next move is to convert the conservative ROI model into a concise, board-ready executive summary with quantified payback period and lock KPIs in a design workshop before senior-management sign-off.

champion.narrative: Meiramgul Kusherbayeva (Manager of Procurement) is the developing champion. She has driven the evaluation from inception (27 Oct 2025 intro call) through every milestone (demos, workshops, ROI validation, pricing refinement, internal approval prep) and has appeared on all eight transcribed buyer calls. She has direct access to the director of procurement and CPO (described the approval chain on 24 Mar and 14 Apr calls) and is personally preparing the one-page CEO presentation (14 Apr call: 'I was requested to make a one-sheet presentation for CEO'). Evidence of advocacy: she pushed to include Zycus and GEP in the evaluation when the original tender invited only Coupa and Oracle (14 Apr call: 'only Coupa and Oracle were invited, excluding other qualified solutions like iValua and GEP, which may lead to higher prices due to lack of competitive options'), explicitly stated 'for me it is important to shorten time period and automize processes... for current situation in procurement market, Ariba is not the tool that can be used further, there are more alternatives who can provide more efficient systems' (28 May call), and requested case studies and reference materials to show advantages of switching from Ariba (24 Mar call: 'I want to show them more advantages and make them choose, not to use in disadvantages manner'). Her engagement is active (last contact 28 May 2026, 29 days ago per pulse). However, she is not yet a strong champion in the Force Management sense: she has not introduced Zycus to the economic buyer (CPO/CEO have not joined a call), has not shared internal docs proving budget or timeline lock, and has not actively coached Zycus on internal politics or competitor moves. She is a coach and an advocate, but sponsorship is still developing. The risk: she is operating at manager level in a 300k enterprise deal with C-level approval required, and the current Ariba extension is unsigned with senior management 'opinion unclear' (14 Apr call). The recommended move is to convert her into a strong champion by equipping her for the CEO presentation (co-author the one-pager, provide reference-client intros, offer to attend the senior-management meeting as her technical partner) and simultaneously run an executive connect (the deal owner's manager with the CPO) to validate her internal positioning and ensure she has the air cover to close.

competition.narrative: Rompetrol is running a live three-way comparison: SAP Ariba (incumbent, under renewal), Zycus, and GEP (peer alternative). Coupa and Oracle were named in the original tender but have faded or been eliminated. The competitive landscape is confirmed across multiple calls and synthesis-map fields, with the relevant stakeholder as the current system and strongest structural threat (renewal path of least resistance), GEP as the active peer finalist Zycus must outscore, and Coupa/Oracle as dormant. On 24 Nov 2025 demo, the team stated 'current procurement solution: SAP Ariba native system' (demo notes). On 24 Mar 2026, Meiramgul requested 'an Excel comparison table for SAP Ariba vs Zycus with AI, integration, sourcing and process parameters' and noted 'only Coupa and Oracle were invited' in the original tender but 'concern regarding tender process, as only Coupa and Oracle were invited, excluding other qualified solutions like iValua and GEP' led procurement to expand the evaluation (24 Mar call). On 14 Apr 2026, she confirmed she is preparing a one-page presentation for the CEO showing 'annual subscription, implementation, and per-model cost for Zycus and for GEP' (14 Apr call). On 28 May 2026, she described attending a recent SAP conference in Madrid where colleagues 'said everything works very nice' but noted 'I'm a little bit suspicious on the level they are presenting; my colleagues are believing them and want to keep Ariba, and I want to make the to-be process map that shows what Zycus can really do' (28 May call). She explicitly stated 'SAP Ariba charges a lot of costs, and the result is not what we are expecting... if people implementing 8 years, oh my god' (28 May call). GEP is the live peer comparison (both vendors shortlisted for final ROI and exec presentation). Coupa and Oracle were in the original RFP but dropped or deprioritized (no mention after March). How Zycus wins: faster implementation than Ariba's multi-year cycles (emphasized on every ROI call), lower TCO (Asmita's conservative model shows €6M year-1 vs Ariba's renewal cost), intrinsic AI vs Ariba's bolted-on Anthropic partnership (Meiramgul: 'AI integration must be intrinsic to eProcurement, not a separate module', 24 Mar call), and modular flexibility to phase by country/module (20 Jan, 14 Apr calls). The wedge is automation and speed; Ariba is semi-manual and slow, GEP is feature-comparable but Zycus has stronger AI orchestration and faster time-to-value. Meiramgul is the internal advocate pushing the switch; her colleagues and senior management are the Ariba incumbency bias to overcome.

identify_pain.narrative: Rompetrol's pain is confirmed, multi-faceted, and owned by procurement operations (Meiramgul, Dorina) with IT complexity (new IT director) as a complicating factor. The specific operational pain articulated by the buyer across calls: (1) Chaotic intake and fragmented request handling—users must hunt across systems ('Who can approve the request, where's my PO, do I have a contract with a vendor—you have to look everywhere and ask people, very cumbersome', intro call 27 Oct 2025, speaker Meiramgul). (2) No orchestration or automation in SAP Ariba—'everything is manual, everything can be changed during the process, it can't be done like that... semi-automized but not fully automized' (28 May call, Meiramgul). (3) Data silos across procurement, sourcing, and invoicing create multiple failure points and no visibility ('limited visibility and control over spend across 20+ affiliated companies', demo 24 Nov 2025). (4) Supplier interactions manual via email with no portal integration ('sourcing and award communications managed manually via email chains', 5 Dec call). (5) Contract/invoice disconnect—'POs initiated in Ariba, invoices in ERP, no linkage... if it is a frame contract, it doesn't come back saying the contract is executed and you have several invoices; we can't see' (28 May call, Meiramgul). (6) Long SAP Ariba implementation cycles and high cost with limited ROI ('Ariba's semi-automation, high implementation costs, and lengthy deployments drive exploration of more efficient alternatives', 27 Mar call synthesis). The pain owner is Meiramgul Kusherbayeva (Manager of Procurement), who is staffing the evaluation and has appeared on all eight transcribed calls. Dorina Aruxandei (Strategic Procurement Manager) co-owns contract and supplier-management pain. The new IT director (joined recently, 28 May call reference) owns integration complexity and SAP migration risk. The compelling event is the unsigned Ariba contract extension sitting on hold while senior management decides whether to renew or replace (14 Apr, 28 May calls). Pain is confirmed and acute; quantified impact is modeled (ROI workshop 27 Mar) but not yet board-approved.

paper_process.narrative: The contracting and paper process is partially scoped but not yet formalized. Integration with the relevant stakeholder (current) and future S/4HANA migration is a known critical path item. On 5 Dec 2025, Meiramgul requested 'implementation plan for SAP ECC and upcoming S/4HANA integration covering master data flows, with Dorina to supply missing fields for technical evaluation' and asked Zycus to 'arrange a technical session with the integration team to walk through SAP ECC and S/4HANA processes and master data synchronization' (5 Dec call). On 20 Jan 2026, the team confirmed Azure SSO integration and asked for 'DocuSign integration for electronic signatures' and clarified 'integration with the relevant stakeholder to sync contract and supplier master information back to SAP' (20 Jan call). On 28 May 2026, Meiramgul described SAP Ariba's current gaps: 'POs initiated in iRequest/Ariba, invoices generated in ERP, with no visibility or linkage in procurement platform' and requested 'digital signature integration as used in Kazakhstan tool, eliminating manual signature steps' (28 May call). Legal and InfoSec review has not been mentioned; no MSA or SOW discussions are on record. No procurement channel or SI/partner involvement is documented. The Current_Contract_Expiration__c field (Ariba renewal) is unpopulated in SF Q2 pull, but multiple calls reference the Ariba contract extension as unsigned and pending senior-management decision (14 Apr, 28 May calls). Rompetrol is evaluating Zycus and GEP as direct replacements; the paper process will likely follow standard enterprise SaaS terms (MSA + SOW), but it has not been kicked off. The recommended move is to initiate the MSA review and InfoSec questionnaire in parallel with pricing finalization so legal does not gate closure once commercial is approved, and to schedule the promised SAP integration deep-dive session with the Zycus integration team before RFI submission.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is a multi-stage internal approval ladder with procurement management (Meiramgul, Dorina, Daniela) driving technical validation, IT director approval on integration and master data, director of procurement + CPO review on ROI and strategic fit, and final C-level (CEO) commercial sign-off. The call sequence itself maps the process: intro and discovery (27 Oct 2025) → full platform demo (24 Nov 2025) → deep-dive on contract/risk/supplier modules (5 Dec 2025) → integration and support scoping (20 Jan 2026) → ROI model validation and budget estimate (24 Mar, 27 Mar 2026) → pricing refinement and one-page exec summary prep (14 Apr 2026) → technical specification finalization and RFI release (28 May 2026, in progress). On 28 May 2026, Meiramgul described the upcoming gates: 'next week meeting with CPO and IT department director, then I will send RFI form in 2-3 weeks, then I will make analysis and understand how we will go on' (28 May call). She also noted the new IT director is now involved and reviewing the to-be process map (28 May call). On 14 Apr 2026, she confirmed 'I was requested to make a one-sheet presentation with identification of annual subscription, implementation cost, and cost per model for Zycus and GEP for CEO mainly' and stated the tender originally included only Coupa and Oracle, but procurement pushed to add Zycus and GEP as qualified alternatives (14 Apr call). On 24 Mar 2026, she confirmed 'head and director of procurement will review before escalation to C-level' (24 Mar call). Approval chain: Meiramgul/Dorina/Daniela (technical validation complete) → IT director (integration approval pending) → procurement director + CPO (ROI approval pending) → CEO (commercial approval pending). The current Ariba contract extension is unsigned and 'on hold' while senior management deliberates (14 Apr call: 'everything is in hold manner'). The next gate is the CPO/IT meeting (week of 3 Jun 2026 per 28 May call), followed by RFI finalization, then senior-management presentation. No formal RFP/selection committee; this is a direct negotiation between Rompetrol procurement and Zycus (with GEP as the peer alternative for comparison).

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's decision criteria are explicitly visible across the call sequence and center on five core requirements: full S2P automation with AI orchestration to eliminate manual effort, integration simplicity with the relevant stakeholder (current) and future S/4HANA migration, lower total cost of ownership versus SAP Ariba, faster implementation than Ariba's multi-year cycles, and modular flexibility to phase rollout by country and use case. On the 24 Nov 2025 demo, Meiramgul and Dorina asked detailed questions on supplier discovery, two-envelope bidding, configurable approval workflows, and Teams integration for adoption, and described demo features as 'amazing' with 'ease of use and Teams integration praised as critical for adoption' (demo notes, 24 Nov 2025). On 5 Dec 2025, they validated contract clause reuse, repository alerts to replace manual Mariba exports, and supplier prequalification scoring to restrict sourcing invites (deep-dive call, 5 Dec 2025). On 20 Jan 2026, Dorina compared Certinal e-signature versus DocuSign, asking 'if it will have more advantages and will be cheaper, why not to try?' (20 Jan call). On 24 Mar 2026, Meiramgul demanded 'AI integration must be intrinsic to eProcurement, not a separate module' and requested an Excel comparison table of Zycus vs SAP Ariba covering 'AI capabilities, integration with ERP, sourcing-to-invoice workflows' plus a one-page executive slide with 'annual subscription, implementation fees, and model costs for Zycus versus competitors' (24 Mar call). On 14 Apr 2026, she confirmed scope as 'sourcing, contract management, supplier management, and POs' and stated 'compare the basic things we have in Ariba without AI; if you have AI built into sourcing module, mark that as included; optional = negotiation agent and agentic AI add-ons' (14 Apr call). On 28 May 2026, she is finalizing a to-be process map by module with 'objectives, activities, deliverables' and an RFI form to formalize technical requirements (28 May call). The criteria are documented, validated through six months of structured use-case reviews, and Zycus is positioned as meeting or exceeding them—pending final pricing and senior-management appetite.

champion_strength.summary: Meiramgul Kusherbayeva is a developing champion with strong technical advocacy but incomplete executive access and no proof of internal political capital. She has driven every milestone since October 2025, explicitly advocates for Zycus over Ariba, and is personally preparing the CEO presentation. However, she is a Manager of Procurement (not director or VP level) in a 300k deal requiring C-level approval, has not introduced Zycus to the CPO or CEO despite 8 months of validation, and has not shared internal docs proving budget lock or timeline commitment. Her sentiment is strongly positive, her effort is consistent, but her influence at the executive layer is unproven. Strength = developing. At risk: yes—she is staffing a complex enterprise decision alone without visible executive air cover, and senior management's opinion is 'unclear' with the Ariba extension 'on hold' per her own words (14 Apr call). The next move is to equip her for the CEO presentation (co-author the one-pager, supply reference-client intros, offer to co-present) and simultaneously run an executive connect (the deal owner's manager) with the CPO to validate her positioning and ensure she has the sponsorship to close.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers to raise close probability now: (1) Executive engagement—CPO/CEO have not joined a call despite 8+ months of validation; an executive connect (the deal owner's manager) with the CPO validates internal positioning, timeline, and budget appetite before the CEO presentation and prevents Meiramgul from presenting alone into a cold room. (2) Competitive differentiation ammunition—Meiramgul is preparing the CEO one-pager comparing Zycus vs GEP vs Ariba; supply her with a crisp 3-slide deck (AI orchestration edge, proven SAP-to-Zycus migrations with named references, implementation speed + TCO advantage) and offer to co-author or co-present so she has technical backup in the room. (3) Close-plan mutual accountability—the current Ariba extension is unsigned and senior management is deliberating; convert the abstract evaluation into a joint close plan with named milestones (RFI response due X, CPO approval gate Y, CEO presentation Z, MSA kickoff W) and mutual commitments (Zycus delivers pricing + integration session by date A, Rompetrol confirms budget + timeline by date B) to create urgency and surface any hidden blockers before they delay past year-end.

competitive_position.summary: Rompetrol is running a live three-way comparison: SAP Ariba (incumbent, semi-manual, high-cost renewal path), Zycus (AI-native, faster implementation, lower TCO), and GEP (peer finalist, feature-comparable). Coupa and Oracle were in the original tender but faded post-March. SAP Ariba is the structural threat (renewal inertia, colleagues favor keeping it per 28 May call) but is exposed on automation gaps, cost, and implementation speed. GEP is the active peer Zycus must outscore on AI orchestration and time-to-value. Meiramgul is the internal advocate pushing the switch; senior management opinion is unclear and the unsigned Ariba extension sits on hold. Zycus wins on intrinsic AI, modular phasing, and proven SAP-to-Zycus migrations (reference clients requested 24 Mar). The single strongest current threat is SAP Ariba incumbency bias, not GEP feature parity—overcoming internal inertia and securing executive sponsorship is the critical path.

Not under-positioned; this is the wedge. Score: Strong. Under-positioned: No.
